title: Deodorant on Cotton
timeEstimate: 10–30 minutes for fresh white marks; chronic yellowing needs the sweat-stain protocol
stainChemistry: Two different deodorant stains live under the same word: (1) FRESH white marks — the waxy/oily base plus aluminum salts that rub off onto cotton as you pull a shirt over antiperspirant-dry skin. Typically goes away with one pass. (2) CHRONIC yellowing — aluminum-protein complex that builds up over many wash cycles when sweat and antiperspirant react in the fiber over time. That's a separate problem (see sweat-stain-cotton). This card is the fresh-white-mark path. Cotton tolerates IPA, NSD, hot wash.
whyThisWorks: Fresh deodorant is a wax + oil + aluminum-salt mix. IPA or POG dissolves the wax-oil carrier. NSD emulsifies residual antiperspirant. Rinse cool. Hot wash finishes. The key ordering is solvent BEFORE water — water alone beads off the wax and leaves the stain behind.
defaultAssumption: Most 'deodorant on cotton' calls are the fresh white-mark variant — dressing after antiperspirant, deodorant transferred onto the shirt. Chronic yellow armpit stains are a different chemistry (sweat-stain-cotton). Read the complaint: white smudges = this card; yellow set-in = sweat-stain-cotton.

spottingProtocol.technique: Rub the marks with the fabric itself
spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean section of the same garment, or a pair of nylon stockings
spottingProtocol.instruction: Fresh white deodorant marks often rub off without any chemistry. Rub the mark gently with a clean section of the same fabric (fabric-on-fabric friction lifts the wax), or use a balled-up pair of nylon stockings. This alone clears most fresh transfers and saves the chemistry for cases that need it.

spottingProtocol.agent: IPA or POG on a White Cloth
spottingProtocol.equipment: Isopropyl alcohol 90%+ OR POG on clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: If the mark persists: tamp with IPA or POG on a white cloth. Solvent dissolves the wax + oil base. Flip cloth frequently. Never pour — tamp only. Cotton tolerates both solvents at room temp.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. NSD emulsifies the residual aluminum-salt film the solvent freed from the fiber. Flush cool from the back.

Rinse cool until water runs clear. Launder hot per care label. Fresh deodorant clears fully in one cycle; if yellow shadow appears over time, the stain has transitioned to chronic sweat-stain chemistry — see the sweat-stain-cotton protocol.

steps: 1. Dry rub with fabric-on-fabric or stockings. Many fresh marks clear here alone.
2. IPA or POG on a white cloth. Tamp, not pour.
3. NSD. Emulsify residual aluminum salts. Cool flush.
4. Cool rinse + hot wash per care label.
5. Chronic yellowing → escalate to sweat-stain-cotton protocol (different chemistry).
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Solvent before water — water alone beads off the wax base
Fresh white marks vs chronic yellow are different stains — don't apply this protocol to chronic yellowing

homeSolutions.agent: Try Fabric Rub First — Often Works
homeSolutions.instruction: Before chemistry: rub the white deodorant mark with a clean section of the same shirt. The friction of fabric-on-fabric lifts the wax without any product. An old pair of nylon stockings balled up works even better. This clears most fresh deodorant transfers in seconds and is the step most people skip.

homeSolutions.agent: Rubbing Alcohol on a White Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: If the rub-off didn't clear it: dab 70%+ rubbing alcohol (drugstore isopropyl) on a clean white cloth, press onto the mark, flip cloth frequently. The alcohol dissolves the wax and oil in the deodorant. Cotton is fine with it.

Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a fingertip or soft toothbrush. Dawn lifts any aluminum-salt residue the alcohol freed. Rinse warm — warm is OK here (no protein is holding anything together yet).

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If a yellow shadow is emerging instead of a fresh white mark, that's chronic sweat-stain chemistry — see the sweat-stain-cotton protocol, which has different steps.

Fresh deodorant marks happen when you pull a shirt over antiperspirant that hasn't fully dried. Let antiperspirant dry 1–2 minutes before dressing and the white marks stop appearing. If white marks keep happening on the same shirts, switch to a non-staining clear antiperspirant formula.

If the deodorant on this shirt has built up over many cycles and is now yellow instead of white, this protocol won't fully clear it. Run the sweat-stain-cotton protocol (vinegar soak + OxiClean + peroxide + sun) instead. If that plateaus, take it to a dry cleaner.

steps: 1. Try fabric-on-fabric rub first — lifts most fresh deodorant without product.
2. Rubbing alcohol on a white cloth if the mark persists.
3. Dawn + warm water to clear residual.
4. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
5. Let antiperspirant dry 1–2 min before dressing to prevent recurrence.
6. Chronic yellowing → run sweat-stain-cotton protocol instead.
products: An old pair of nylon stockings (best) OR the same garment's fabric
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop this protocol and switch to sweat-stain-cotton if the mark is yellow instead of white. Escalate to a dry cleaner if both protocols plateau.
warnings: Solvent before water — water alone doesn't lift the wax
Fresh white marks and chronic yellowing need different protocols
Never mix alcohol with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never assume yellow armpit stains are the same as fresh white deodorant marks — different chemistry
Never pour alcohol on fabric — tamp with a cloth
Never use chlorine bleach on white deodorant marks — dulls cotton over time with no benefit here
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates IPA, POG, NSD, hot wash
Dyed cotton: test alcohol on inside seam if concerned about dye stability
Heat-durable fiber — hot wash is the finisher

materialWarnings: Solvent before water — wax base beads off water
White marks vs yellow stains = different protocols
Let antiperspirant dry before dressing to prevent

escalation.when: The mark is yellow rather than white (chronic sweat-stain chemistry), or the fresh-mark protocol plateaus on a shirt that's been worn through many cycles.
escalation.whatToTell: "Deodorant on cotton — [fresh white marks / chronic yellowing]. Home-treated with fabric rub + rubbing alcohol + Dawn. [Mark cleared / yellow remains.]"

contextQuestions: Is the mark white (fresh transfer) or yellow (chronic build-up)?
Was the antiperspirant applied just before dressing?
Has this shirt yellowed across many wash cycles?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh white marks on a shirt worn once = this protocol. Yellow armpit stains across many wash cycles = run sweat-stain-cotton, which addresses the aluminum-protein complex properly.
